speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 10, 1996. The survivor is Slava Fintel, and this is tape three. You said that you had been captured by this drunken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was in his-- in the back, and he was apparently very drunk. He start driving. And I decided to try-- which apparently, I succeeded. I'm here. I rolled down from his sled, and I rolled and rolled and rolled, and I rolled into the river. It was-- the top was frozen, but I fell into it. It was just the beginning of-- and I swam through it, and I went up as fast as I could, and I start running towards the house. I came to the house. It was dark, night. The whole family was sitting around the table. They were quite a large family. And among them, my sister [? Tzila ?] and Piotr. And I saw them in the window. And by some kind of miracle, I just knocked. And I saw Piotr looking at me, and the next thing I knew, I was carried by Piotr on his arms to a place where they used to keep their pigs. It was warm there, and there was a huge, huge kettle that they used to cook the food for all the pigs. He put on top a big board and put me there, and tried to see what he can do with me. But that time, the clothes that I was wearing were stuck to my skin. My feet were all frozen and bruised. My hair was all frozen in this red, awful, burgundy kind of icicles. And what I remember, what he did-- he took a big linen. He dunked it in some kind of fat or something. I don't know, because when-- he undressed me and covered me, and rolled me in, in this particular sheet of this warm oil, or maybe-- I don't know what it was. And I lay there. He fed me. He gave me to drink. But apparently he thought that I will never survive. So he tried to keep me away from my sister, and he didn't say anything. He kept me there for several days, or maybe more than a week, because I came to. I start eating. I start drinking. He cut off my hair with a scissors where you cut sheep. And you could imagine how it looked. It was like in steps. But I never saw myself because I never saw a mirror. And I-- he felt, apparently, that I'm going to live, and he decided to tell my sister. And our reunion-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had also to tell her that I'm the only one. Nobody escaped. [SIGHS] It was very-- it was so-- [SIGHS] I felt that it is more than a miracle that I myself survived this ordeal. By that time, Piotr let-- somehow he let know my brothers. By that time, partisans were organized. My brothers, instead of being with the Jewish brigades that they tried to do themselves-- at that time, there were already many, many otriads that were organized, and my two brothers belonged to those. Piotr let them know that not only [? Tzila ?] is with him, but also that I survived, and that I'm there. One of the nights, they came. And this was, again, a repetition of our reunion and explaining what happened, and that I'm the only one survivor. They couldn't stay long because they belonged, at that time, in the partisans, and they were really like soldiers. It was a regime. And when they came, they took just one night leave of absence, and they only traveled in the night. And from that time on, I was hidden, again, in-- they made a space for us. And this was the night when my brothers came, and they were frightened that not too long I can be hidden in the hay. So in the night, they dug-- in one of the parts of the barn, they dug a pit. And they put in there candles, and some food, and a blanket, and thought if the Germans will come to look for Jews-- because this was a regular occurrence. They would go to people because some people would tell that some Jews are hiding. And they would come and find them and liquidate them. So they thought that if we would even-- we will see-- Piotr would see that some-- somebody's approaching their farm, that we will both jump into this pit and put over our head-- it was a box of sand with-- so done that nobody could ever-- even if they would take their bayonets and pierce through. Because this is what they did. They used to come with bayonets and look in the-- in the haystacks with bayonets, to look if somebody's hiding there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had this-- they felt it secure enough that we would-- if the time would come, that we would use it. They left back to the partisans. And then once more, they came to visit us. And it occurred to me, I was hidden by myself in that haystack in the daytime. Lots of times, [? Tzila ?] would join me, but most of the time, I was alone. And it was very hard, as before.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mice had a ball. I mean they-- They kept biting and they kept-- it was impossible. And when spring came, and for the third time my brothers came to visit me, I begged them that I would like to come with them. Also, in the same vicinity where the partisans were, lots of Jewish families, survivors, organized. And they lived there in bunkers. They dug from the ground, and it was bunkers. And the food they would get that the Jewish boys, the partisans, would go in the night and steal and bring for them, and this how they survived. And I heard about it, and I knew already from my brothers. And I started begging them that please, they should take me out of there. When [? Tzila ?] heard that I am going to leave, she decided that she will also leave. But she was a grown person. She was-- I was too young, and she was an adult. So when she decided that she will go to the partisans, Piotr arranged for her to get a gun, and he followed her. He was in love with her, and he followed her. He left the family and he arranged for himself and for her. And they left, and they went to the partisans. My brother Zalman came, took me, brought me to a group of Jewish family, and parked me there for a time being, until he will arrange for me to get some kind of gun or anything that I could be taking to the-- to the partisans, to-- with him. It-- we stayed-- I stayed with this family for several months. And the Germans decided that they have enough of the partisans, and they couldn't take it because they were fighting very fiercely. They were burning the bridges, and they were burning everything they saw. That food would be sent, like cattle, they would liquidate that. And the Germans decided that this is the only way, that they must liquidate the partisans. And they arranged a very thorough combing of the woods-- also by planes and by artillery-- to get rid of the partisans. The partisans ran towards a different way. But the Jews who were hiding didn't know anywhere to run. But they were very acquainted with the woods and the vicinity. And I among them, we start running. It was a very big family. They were actually two families. And we ran. And the-- the shooting, the bombing, it was-- I never thought that we will survive that. But this wasn't as-- I-- I wasn't that upset, because I thought, if I'm going to be killed by a bomb, it's better than running like a-- like a scared animal and being shot by the Germans and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we ran. And again, we all ran, and we came to the swamps. And we all, again, got into the swamps. The Germans never came there, neither the-- the local collaborators. They never went to the swamps, because they thought they-- nobody could survive. But when in need, you can. We-- we were in the swamps for-- until it got very quiet and it-- they retreated. And then we got out of there, and again, we had the problem of cleaning ourselves and this. And one of the men, his name was [? [NON-ENGLISH]. ?] Although he was a huge man, but his nickname was [NON-ENGLISH], because he was so huge, so they made him that he's tiny.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought us to an abandoned partisan location where they had-- where they had those dugouts. And we stayed there. But there was no food. None. And we were very, very frightened that we will be caught, because the gentiles who were collaborating, they are-- they were very knowledgeable in all those hiding places. And we were afraid we will still be caught. But we had to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- once they went and they caught a cow on a pasture. They brought the cow and they killed it, but they didn't have any-- any-- how to they killed it, I don't know. But they had knives. And they cut out from-- they just opened up a part of the cow's skin, and they cut out certain meats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of the others used to eat it raw. And the only thing they had was flour that they stole from the barns, from the gentile. And in the night, they used to make a fire, and they used to cook this-- this flour and water. And it was like a-- like a gooey kind of stuff. And each of us used to come with a leaf, and he used to give us somewhat on the leaf. And he used to la-- I mean scream at me and said to me, you always find the biggest leaf because you think you will get more of the food. As I said before, salt was so scarce. But without salt, you became, like, swollen. And he used to give-- you had to take out your tongue like this, and he used to come and give a drop of salt to each person. And this is how it-- it helped us not to become sick. He was also afraid that he cannot keep so many mouths to feed. And I really understand. And he was also afraid that I might be caught. So they decided when-- several times, they wanted to go and to steal of the-- of the gentiles' food, that they will take me with. And this way, I will not know how to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- when they left for quite a bit, we walked, and walked, and walked. And then he put something on my eyes. I shouldn't see where we were. And he brought me to a certain place and left me there, and they ran away. And it took me quite a while to undo my-- from my eyes, the thing that they put on. And somehow, by some kind of miracle, I did come back to the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, that time they knew that they have to keep me. And they took me for a second time to go in the night to bring food. It was night. And I had boots that I took from a dead person, but didn't have any ropes or something. So the shoes were big on me, and when I was walking, I used to lose them. I didn't have any-- any way to close them up. And I had to follow them. And he put in a big bag on my back, and they put lots of potatoes there. And when I was walking-- it was so heavy that the potatoes-- I would fall and I would lose my shoes. So quietly, I used to-- I used to try to find somewhere, a hole or something, to remove-- potatoes should fall out. It shouldn't be that-- that heavy on me. And I stayed with them like that for about several weeks. I was always so hungry. So hungry. And in the night, I used to crawl to the cow, and I used to find some kind of pointy thing and just take out little pieces of this meat and chew. And I-- my whole body became with boils, maybe because no food or vitamins or whatever. I had such boils over my body. And I almost gave up, because I didn't have any strength. I didn't have anybody. Nobody. But my brother knew that he left me with this family, and he decided that he's going to look for us. By some kind of miracle, he found out where we were. And one day I really gave up, because I didn't think that it was worthwhile living anymore. My brother Zalman appeared like an angel from nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I saw him, I was lying on-- on a bunch of dirt. I looked, and I-- I didn't believe that it could-- it could really be. And then when he picked me up, and I kept saying, is it really-- is it really you? Is it really possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] He took me on his back. I couldn't walk. The boils were so big with pus, my-- my whole body. He put me on his back. We walked for a very long time, and we came to the-- he took me to the partisans. He organized before I came there that I had already a weapon, because otherwise, they would never take you. The first few months, I tried to recuperate from the wounds, because they had quite-- they had a nurse. They had medicine. They were supplied by planes. Once in a while, a plane would come and throw many supplies, and also medical supplies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who-- whose planes they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were Russians. This is-- they were connected with Russia. This was already organized. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were-- where were you, if you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were in the [BELARUSIAN]. This is the name. And the brigada where I belonged to the partisans was Zhukov Brigade. [SIGHS] I was working first in the kitchen and helping the other women to peel potatoes and other preparations of food. But after a short while, they used me for other things. When the otriad, the brigade, went to-- to do-- I don't know-- to do-- I don't know how to say that-- actions. When they went to do the actions, that they had to destroy some train, or destroy some food, or knowing that they are transferring the Germans, they-- I would be taken with them. They gave me a rope, and if one of the soldiers was wounded, and-- I had to crawl, put that rope as possible around the person, and crawl back and bring that rope, that they could retrieve that wounded person. And this went on like that. Then, when they needed, they also put me on a patrol. And before the-- if anybody would get close, you had to ask their-- a certain-- it was-- you had to ask a-- a certain name-- word, that it was connected, that if they did not know, you were permitted to shoot them. And this how I spent my time in the partisans. It was summer, and we knew that the Germans began to retreat. And it was like in the air. We knew that soon, possibly, we are going to be liberated. It was in June when we were liberated. And my brother and myself went to our city. When we came there, of course, our homes were burned. But one of the houses of my cousin was still standing, and we went there. After a very short time, they caught my brother and they sent him to the front. By the way, being in the partisans, before my brother came to retrieve me, my older brother was with him. He was wounded in the knee. And Zalman, my brother, organized-- of course, you had to bribe and do many things-- and they sent him out one of the planes to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Zalman came for me, to retrieve me, my brother was already saved. This what we hoped, that he was in Russia, saved. Because he was useless to the partisans because he-- his knee was shot, and he couldn't walk or he couldn't do anything. So we-- my brother and I were liberated. We came to our town. And after a very short time, they took him to-- to Russia front. I did not know if [? Tzila, ?] my sister, where she was or if she-- if she survived. But many, many people start coming to the town. And not too many, but the people who survived, everybody came to look for others who maybe, by some miracle, survived too.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there, and one of the newcomers was my sister. Piotr was also taken to the army. [? Tzila's ?] friend, Piotr, was also taken there. But I don't know how it happened that my brother got out-- Zalman-- and he came to us, to our town, and he became-- he start working for the Russians. And he-- we start searching for my brother in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a tape break now.
